name: South Africa
tagline: From the cradle of humankind and the early San, Khoekhoe and Bantu-speaking kingdoms, through Dutch and British colonisation, the trauma of apartheid, and the birth of a non-racial democracy in 1994.
summary: South Africa holds one of the deepest human pasts on Earth: the limestone caves of the Cradle of Humankind near Johannesburg have yielded hominin fossils millions of years old, and the San and Khoekhoe peoples have inhabited the subcontinent for tens of thousands of years. From the early centuries of the Common Era, Bantu-speaking farmers and herders spread across the eastern half of the region, building societies that culminated in states such as Mapungubwe. In 1652 the Dutch East India Company planted a refreshment station at the Cape, beginning a long history of European settlement, dispossession, and slavery. Britain seized the Cape in the early nineteenth century; the Mfecane convulsed the interior as the Zulu kingdom rose under Shaka; Voortrekkers founded Boer republics; and the discovery of diamonds at Kimberley in 1867 and gold on the Witwatersrand in 1886 transformed the region, leading to the devastating South African War and, in 1910, the Union of South Africa. After 1948 the National Party built the brutal system of apartheid, met by a long liberation struggle marked by Sharpeville, Soweto, and the imprisonment of Nelson Mandela. That struggle culminated in the first democratic, non-racial election of April 1994, when Mandela became president of a free South Africa governed today under one of the world's most admired constitutions.

founding.label: Union of South Africa
founding.detail: The modern state took its first political shape on 31 May 1910, when the British colonies of the Cape, Natal, Transvaal and Orange River were joined into the self-governing Union of South Africa under the South Africa Act passed by the British Parliament in 1909. Yet this founding rests on a far deeper past: the Sterkfontein and Swartkrans caves of the Cradle of Humankind preserve hominin fossils millions of years old, and the San and Khoekhoe have lived in the region for tens of thousands of years. The 1910 Union, however, was built on the exclusion of the Black majority from political power, a foundation that hardened into apartheid after 1948. The true birth of the modern nation came on 27 April 1994, when South Africans of all races voted together for the first time in a fully democratic, non-racial election, bringing Nelson Mandela and the African National Congress to power and ending more than three centuries of white minority rule.

quickFacts.label: Union founded
quickFacts.value: Union of South Africa formed on 31 May 1910 from four British colonies

quickFacts.label: Democracy
quickFacts.value: First democratic, non-racial election held 26–29 April 1994; Mandela inaugurated 10 May 1994

quickFacts.label: Human origins
quickFacts.value: Cradle of Humankind fossil sites preserve hominins millions of years old

quickFacts.label: Government
quickFacts.value: Constitutional democracy under the 1996 Constitution; three capitals (Pretoria, Cape Town, Bloemfontein)

quickFacts.label: Languages
quickFacts.value: Twelve official languages, including isiZulu, isiXhosa, Afrikaans, English and, since 2023, South African Sign Language

quickFacts.label: Apartheid
quickFacts.value: Legalised racial segregation from 1948 until democratic elections in 1994

eras.title: Early peoples: San, Khoekhoe and the Bantu-speaking kingdoms
eras.standfirst: From hominin ancestors in the Cradle of Humankind to the hunter-gatherer San, the herding Khoekhoe, and the Bantu-speaking farmers who built states such as Mapungubwe, southern Africa nurtured human societies across an immense span of time.
body: Southern Africa is one of the great theatres of human origins. In the dolomitic caves at Sterkfontein, Swartkrans and Kromdraai north-west of Johannesburg — collectively the Cradle of Humankind, inscribed by UNESCO in 1999 — palaeontologists have recovered an extraordinary record of early hominins, including the celebrated Australopithecus africanus skull known as Mrs Ples and the near-complete skeleton called Little Foot. These finds, spanning millions of years, helped establish Africa as the birthplace of humanity.

The oldest enduring inhabitants of the region are the San, hunter-gatherers whose ancestors have lived in southern Africa for tens of thousands of years and whose rock art adorns shelters across the subcontinent. Around two thousand years ago they were joined by the Khoekhoe (Khoikhoi), pastoralists who herded sheep and cattle and moved seasonally in search of grazing. Together often called the Khoisan, these peoples shared the land long before any farming societies or European ships arrived.

From the early centuries of the Common Era, Bantu-speaking farmers — ancestors of today's Nguni, Sotho-Tswana, Venda and Tsonga peoples — spread southward into the eastern, better-watered half of the region, bringing iron-working, cattle and crops such as sorghum and millet. By the second millennium they had built substantial societies. The most striking was the kingdom of Mapungubwe near the Limpopo, which flourished from about 1220 to 1300 as southern Africa's first state-level society, trading gold and ivory to the Indian Ocean coast and leaving behind exquisite gold artefacts such as a small gold-foil rhinoceros.

events.title: The Cradle of Humankind
events.summary: The Sterkfontein and Swartkrans caves preserve hominin fossils spanning millions of years, including Mrs Ples and Little Foot, marking southern Africa as a key site of human origins; the area was inscribed by UNESCO in 1999.

events.title: San and Khoekhoe of the subcontinent
events.summary: The San, hunter-gatherers present for tens of thousands of years, were joined around two thousand years ago by the herding Khoekhoe, together forming the long-established Khoisan societies of the region.

events.title: Bantu-speaking farmers spread south
events.summary: Bantu-speaking communities bringing iron-working, cattle and cultivated crops settled the eastern half of southern Africa, ancestors of the region's Nguni, Sotho-Tswana, Venda and Tsonga peoples.

events.title: The kingdom of Mapungubwe
events.summary: Near the Limpopo, Mapungubwe rose as southern Africa's first state-level society, trading gold and ivory to the Indian Ocean coast and producing famed gold artefacts before its decline around 1300.

figures.name: The San (Bushmen)
figures.life: present for tens of thousands of years
figures.blurb: Southern Africa's oldest enduring inhabitants, hunter-gatherers whose deep knowledge of the land and whose rock art across the subcontinent record one of humanity's longest continuous cultural traditions.

pullquote.text: The caves of the Cradle of Humankind hold fossils millions of years old, marking southern Africa as one of the birthplaces of humanity.

eras.title: The Dutch Cape Colony and the frontier
eras.standfirst: In 1652 the Dutch East India Company founded a refreshment station at the Cape, beginning European settlement, the importation of enslaved people, the dispossession of the Khoekhoe, and the expansion of trekboers into a violent frontier.
body: On 6 April 1652 a small Dutch East India Company (VOC) expedition under Jan van Riebeeck landed at the foot of Table Mountain and built a fort to supply passing ships with fresh produce on the long voyage between Europe and Asia. What began as a victualling station soon became a colony of settlement. Company employees were released as free burghers to farm, and from 1657 the colony expanded inland, encroaching on the grazing lands of the Khoekhoe.

Settlement brought conflict and catastrophe for the indigenous peoples. The Khoekhoe lost land and cattle to the expanding colony and were devastated by warfare and by a smallpox epidemic in 1713 that shattered their societies. To meet its demand for labour, the VOC imported enslaved people from across the Indian Ocean world — from Madagascar, East Africa, India and the Indonesian archipelago — whose descendants, together with the Khoekhoe and incoming Europeans, formed the diverse population of the western Cape and shaped its language and culture.

Over the eighteenth century the colony's frontier pushed eastward and northward as semi-nomadic cattle farmers, the trekboers, sought new pastures. By the 1770s they had reached the Great Fish River, where they collided with the Xhosa, beginning a long series of frontier wars. A society of settler farmers, enslaved labourers, dispossessed Khoekhoe and indigenous African peoples thus took shape under Company rule, riven by the inequalities that would echo through South African history.

events.title: Van Riebeeck founds the Cape station
events.summary: Jan van Riebeeck landed at Table Bay and established a Dutch East India Company refreshment station, the beginning of permanent European settlement in South Africa.

events.title: Slavery at the Cape
events.summary: The Company began importing enslaved people from Madagascar, East Africa, India and the Indonesian archipelago, founding a slave society whose labour underpinned the colony for nearly two centuries.

events.title: Smallpox devastates the Khoekhoe
events.summary: A smallpox epidemic introduced by passing ships killed great numbers of Khoekhoe, accelerating the loss of their land, herds and independence as the colony expanded.

events.title: First Cape Frontier War
events.summary: As trekboers reached the Great Fish River they clashed with the Xhosa, beginning a century-long series of frontier wars along the colony's eastern edge.

figures.name: Jan van Riebeeck
figures.role: Founder and commander of the Cape station
figures.blurb: Dutch East India Company official who established the refreshment station at the Cape in 1652, opening southern Africa to European settlement and laying the foundations of the Cape Colony.

pullquote.text: What began in 1652 as a victualling station for passing ships became a colony of settlement, dispossession and slavery.

eras.title: British rule, the Mfecane, the Zulu kingdom and the Boer republics
eras.standfirst: Britain seized the Cape, slavery was abolished, the Zulu kingdom rose under Shaka amid the upheavals of the Mfecane, and Voortrekkers left the colony on the Great Trek to found independent Boer republics in the interior.
body: Britain occupied the Cape in 1806 during the Napoleonic Wars and retained it permanently, ushering in a new colonial order. British rule brought the English language, the 1820 Settlers, and reforms that unsettled the established Dutch-speaking colonists — above all the banning of the slave trade and the abolition of slavery itself across the British Empire, which took effect at the Cape on 1 December 1834. Many Boer farmers resented these changes and the colony's eastern-frontier policies.

In the same era the interior was transformed by the Mfecane (in Sesotho, the Difaqane), a period of widespread warfare, migration and state formation in the 1810s and 1820s. Its central engine was the rise of the Zulu kingdom under Shaka, who from about 1816 forged a powerful centralised state and a reorganised army that subjugated neighbouring chiefdoms. The resulting wars and displacements rippled across the subcontinent, founding new kingdoms such as the Ndebele, Sotho and Swazi even as they brought great suffering.

From 1836 thousands of disaffected Boers — the Voortrekkers — left the Cape Colony on the Great Trek, seeking land and independence beyond British control. Moving into the highveld and Natal, they clashed with African kingdoms; at the Battle of Blood River on 16 December 1838 a Voortrekker commando defeated a large Zulu force. Over the following years the trekkers established the independent Boer republics of the Transvaal (South African Republic) and the Orange Free State, which Britain recognised in the 1850s, fracturing the region into competing British colonies, Boer republics and African kingdoms.

events.title: Britain seizes the Cape
events.summary: British forces occupied the Cape Colony during the Napoleonic Wars, beginning lasting British rule confirmed by the Congress of Vienna in 1815.

events.title: Rise of the Zulu kingdom and the Mfecane
events.summary: Shaka built the Zulu into a powerful centralised kingdom with a reorganised army; the wars and migrations of the Mfecane reshaped the political map of southern Africa.

events.title: Abolition of slavery at the Cape
events.summary: Slavery was abolished across the British Empire, freeing enslaved people at the Cape and deepening Boer discontent that helped spark the Great Trek.

events.title: The Great Trek
events.summary: Thousands of Voortrekkers left the Cape Colony for the interior, seeking independence from British rule and founding new Boer communities on lands occupied by African peoples.

events.title: Battle of Blood River
events.summary: A Voortrekker commando under Andries Pretorius defeated a large Zulu army at the Ncome (Blood) River, a battle long mythologised in Afrikaner nationalism and preceding the rise of Boer republics.

figures.name: Shaka
figures.blurb: Founder-king who transformed the Zulu into a dominant military kingdom in the 1810s and 1820s, his conquests central to the upheavals of the Mfecane; he was assassinated by his half-brothers in 1828.

figures.name: Andries Pretorius
figures.blurb: Boer commander who led the Voortrekkers to victory at Blood River in 1838 and helped establish the independent Boer republics; the city of Pretoria is named for his family.

pullquote.text: The rise of the Zulu kingdom and the Mfecane reshaped the political map of southern Africa even as Britain remade the Cape.

eras.title: Diamonds, gold, the South African War and Union
eras.standfirst: The discovery of diamonds at Kimberley and gold on the Witwatersrand turned southern Africa into a magnet for capital and labour, driving imperial ambition, the destructive South African War, and the creation of the Union of South Africa in 1910.
body: The discovery of diamonds near Kimberley in 1867 and of vast gold reefs on the Witwatersrand in 1886 set off a mineral revolution that remade the region. Mining drew immense foreign capital, swelled new cities such as Johannesburg, and created a migrant-labour system that pulled Black workers into the mines under harsh, segregated conditions — laying economic foundations for the racial order to come. The Transvaal's gold made the Boer republics the richest prize in the subcontinent and intensified British imperial designs.

Tensions between Britain and the Boer republics over the goldfields and the rights of immigrant uitlanders erupted into the South African War (Anglo-Boer War) of 1899 to 1902. After early Boer successes, Britain poured in nearly half a million troops; the Boers turned to guerrilla warfare, and the British responded with farm-burning and a system of concentration camps in which tens of thousands of Boer and Black civilians died of disease and malnutrition. The war ended with the Treaty of Vereeniging on 31 May 1902, bringing the republics under the British Crown.

Reconstruction and reconciliation between British and Afrikaner whites led to political union. A National Convention drafted a constitution, enacted by the British Parliament as the South Africa Act of 1909, and on 31 May 1910 the Cape, Natal, Transvaal and Orange River colonies were joined into the self-governing Union of South Africa, with Louis Botha as first prime minister. The Union was a white settlement: the franchise was overwhelmingly reserved for whites, and the Black majority was excluded from the new state's political life — a grievance that prompted the founding of the South African Native National Congress, later the ANC, in 1912.

events.title: Diamonds discovered at Kimberley
events.summary: The discovery of diamonds near the Vaal and Orange rivers triggered a rush to Kimberley, beginning the mineral revolution and drawing capital and migrant labour into the region.

events.title: Gold found on the Witwatersrand
events.summary: The discovery of the world's largest goldfield on the Witwatersrand created Johannesburg, transformed the Transvaal's wealth, and entrenched a segregated migrant-labour system.

events.title: The South African War
events.summary: War between Britain and the Boer republics featured guerrilla campaigns and British concentration camps in which tens of thousands of Boer and Black civilians died.

events.title: Treaty of Vereeniging
events.summary: The peace signed at Pretoria ended the South African War, bringing the Transvaal and Orange Free State under British sovereignty with promises of eventual self-government.

events.title: Union of South Africa
events.summary: The four British colonies were united into the self-governing Union of South Africa under the South Africa Act, a white-controlled state that excluded the Black majority from political power.

figures.name: Cecil John Rhodes
figures.role: Mining magnate and Cape prime minister
figures.blurb: British imperialist who built the De Beers diamond monopoly at Kimberley, became prime minister of the Cape Colony, and championed an expansionist vision whose schemes helped propel the region toward the South African War.

figures.name: Louis Botha
figures.role: First Prime Minister of the Union of South Africa
figures.blurb: Boer general in the South African War who became the first prime minister of the Union in 1910, pursuing reconciliation between British and Afrikaner whites within a state that excluded Black South Africans.

pullquote.text: The Union of 1910 united four colonies into one self-governing state — but built that state upon the exclusion of the Black majority.

eras.title: Apartheid and the liberation struggle
eras.standfirst: After 1948 the National Party built apartheid, a comprehensive legal system of racial domination. Decades of resistance — from the Defiance Campaign and the massacre at Sharpeville to the Soweto uprising and the long imprisonment of Nelson Mandela — finally forced the regime to negotiate its own end.
body: In 1948 the National Party won power and began to construct apartheid, a sweeping legal architecture of racial separation and white supremacy. The Population Registration Act of 1950 classified every person by race; the Group Areas Act of 1950 carved cities into racial zones and drove millions from their homes through forced removals; pass laws controlled the movement of Black South Africans; and the Bantustan system stripped Black citizens of national rights by assigning them to nominally self-governing 'homelands'. Apartheid degraded the dignity and shattered the lives of the Black majority and of Coloured and Indian South Africans across every sphere of existence.

Resistance was sustained and courageous, and it was met with brutal repression. The African National Congress launched the Defiance Campaign in 1952 and, with allies, adopted the Freedom Charter in 1955. On 21 March 1960, police opened fire on a peaceful crowd protesting the pass laws at Sharpeville, killing at least 69 people, many shot in the back; the Sharpeville massacre shocked the world, brought the banning of the ANC and Pan Africanist Congress, and turned the struggle toward armed resistance. Nelson Mandela and other leaders were imprisoned for life at the Rivonia Trial in 1964. On 16 June 1976, the Soweto uprising — sparked by protest against compulsory teaching in Afrikaans — saw schoolchildren shot down by police, galvanising a new generation of resistance.

Through the 1980s, mass township revolt, trade-union militancy, the United Democratic Front, and an intensifying campaign of international sanctions and isolation made apartheid unsustainable. Faced with deepening crisis, President F. W. de Klerk in February 1990 unbanned the ANC and other organisations and, on 11 February 1990, freed Nelson Mandela after more than 27 years in prison. Negotiations, though shadowed by violence, produced an interim constitution and a date for the country's first democratic election — the formal end of apartheid.

events.title: National Party institutes apartheid
events.summary: The National Party won the 1948 election and began enacting apartheid, including the Population Registration Act and Group Areas Act of 1950, codifying racial classification, segregation and forced removals.

events.title: Sharpeville massacre
events.summary: Police fired on a peaceful anti-pass-law protest at Sharpeville, killing at least 69 people and wounding scores more; the atrocity drew global condemnation and led to the banning of the ANC and PAC.

events.title: Rivonia Trial imprisons Mandela
events.summary: Nelson Mandela and fellow leaders of the ANC's armed wing were convicted of sabotage and sentenced to life imprisonment, beginning Mandela's 27 years behind bars.

events.title: Soweto uprising
events.summary: Student protests against compulsory instruction in Afrikaans were met with police gunfire in Soweto, killing many, including children, and igniting nationwide resistance.

events.title: Mandela freed and the ANC unbanned
events.summary: President F. W. de Klerk unbanned the ANC, PAC and other organisations and released Nelson Mandela from prison, opening the way to negotiations that would dismantle apartheid.

figures.name: Nelson Mandela
figures.blurb: ANC leader imprisoned for 27 years for his role in the armed struggle, he became the embodiment of the fight against apartheid and, on his release in 1990, the chief negotiator of South Africa's transition to democracy.

figures.name: Hendrik Verwoerd
figures.role: Architect of apartheid and Prime Minister
figures.blurb: As minister of native affairs and then prime minister, he designed many of apartheid's foundational laws, including 'Bantu education' and the homelands system, before his assassination in 1966.

pullquote.text: At Sharpeville on 21 March 1960, police killed at least 69 people protesting the pass laws — many shot in the back as they fled.

eras.title: Democratic South Africa since 1994
eras.standfirst: On 27 April 1994 South Africans of every race voted together for the first time, electing Nelson Mandela president. A new constitution, the Truth and Reconciliation Commission and the ANC's long dominance defined a democracy still wrestling with inequality and the legacies of apartheid.
body: The first democratic, non-racial election was held over several days in late April 1994, with the principal voting day on 27 April — now commemorated as Freedom Day. Some twenty million people voted, an 86.9 percent turnout, and the African National Congress won decisively with about 62.65 percent of the vote. On 10 May 1994 Nelson Mandela was inaugurated as the first president of a free and democratic South Africa, a moment watched around the world as the symbolic end of apartheid and three centuries of white minority rule.

The young democracy built durable institutions. A final Constitution, signed into law in December 1996 and effective in February 1997, enshrined a justiciable bill of rights and is widely admired as one of the world's most progressive. To confront the crimes of the past, the Truth and Reconciliation Commission, chaired by Archbishop Desmond Tutu, held public hearings from 1996, recording testimony from victims and offering conditional amnesty to perpetrators who fully disclosed politically motivated crimes — a distinctive, contested model of transitional justice.

In the decades since, the ANC has governed continuously, presiding over the extension of housing, water, electricity and social grants to millions, even as South Africa has remained one of the most unequal societies in the world, burdened by high unemployment, persistent poverty, the HIV/AIDS epidemic, and corruption scandals. The 2024 election, in which the ANC lost its parliamentary majority for the first time and entered a government of national unity, marked a new chapter in the country's still-evolving democracy.

events.title: First democratic election
events.summary: South Africans of all races voted together for the first time; the ANC won about 62.65 percent of the vote on an 86.9 percent turnout, ending apartheid rule. The main polling day, 27 April, is marked as Freedom Day.

events.title: Mandela inaugurated as president
events.summary: Nelson Mandela was sworn in as the first president of a democratic South Africa, leading a Government of National Unity and symbolising reconciliation across the country's racial divide.

events.title: Truth and Reconciliation Commission
events.summary: Chaired by Archbishop Desmond Tutu, the TRC began public hearings into apartheid-era human rights abuses, offering conditional amnesty in exchange for full disclosure as a model of transitional justice.

events.title: A new Constitution
events.summary: South Africa's final Constitution, with its justiciable Bill of Rights, was signed into law in 1996 and took effect in February 1997, widely regarded as among the most progressive in the world.

events.title: ANC loses its majority
events.summary: In the 2024 general election the ANC lost the outright parliamentary majority it had held since 1994, leading to a multiparty government of national unity and a new phase of coalition politics.

figures.name: Nelson Mandela
figures.role: First President of democratic South Africa
figures.blurb: Inaugurated in 1994 after 27 years' imprisonment, he led South Africa's peaceful transition, championed reconciliation, and stepped down after a single term, becoming a global symbol of freedom and forgiveness.

figures.name: Desmond Tutu
figures.role: Archbishop and chair of the TRC
figures.blurb: Anglican archbishop and Nobel Peace laureate who gave moral voice to the anti-apartheid cause and chaired the Truth and Reconciliation Commission, coining the vision of South Africa as a 'rainbow nation'.

pullquote.text: On 27 April 1994, for the first time, South Africans of every race stood in the same queues to vote — the birth of the modern nation.

figures.name: Nelson Mandela
figures.role: Liberation leader and first democratic President
figures.blurb: The defining figure of South Africa's liberation, imprisoned for 27 years and then elected the country's first Black president in 1994, leading its transition from apartheid to constitutional democracy.

figures.name: Shaka
figures.blurb: Founder-king of the Zulu kingdom whose military and political revolution in the early nineteenth century reshaped southern Africa during the upheavals of the Mfecane.
